coredata.dc:title: Giant femtosecond nonlinear optical response in bi-metallic GO nanocomposites for photonic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Koushik
Biswas, Subrata
Pramanik, Ashim
Banerjee, Dipanjan
Rao Soma, Venugopal
Kumar Chaudhary, Anil
Kumbhakar, Pathik
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nonlinear Optical Materials
Graphene oxide nanocomposite
Two photon absorption
Three photon absorption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: KM is thankful to UGC, Govt. of India for financial assistance. Authors acknowledge the partial financial supports from Dept. of Science & Biotechnology, Govt. of West Bengal, India, with the project Grant No. 332(Sanc.)/ST/P/S&T/16G-24/2018 dt.06.03.19. Venugopal Rao Soma acknowledges the financial support from DRDO through ACRHEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Visible absorption spectra of (i) GO, (ii) GO_Ag, (iii) GO_Au, (iv) GO_Au_Ag nanocomposites, [Here the red line indicates the position 250 nm.] (v) GQD and (vi) N-GQD. (b) XRD spectra of GO, GO_Au, and GO_Au_Ag nanocomposites. Here position of (011) plane in GO_Au, and GO_Au_Ag is indicated by the black dotted line. Red dotted line also indicates the same for GO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM image of (a) GO, (b) GO_Au, (c) GO_Ag, (d) GO_Au_Ag composites, (e) GQD, (f) HRTEM image of GQD, (g) N-GQD, and (h) Enlarged view of a single N-GQD.
EDS spectra for the synthesized GO_Au (a) and GO_Au_Ag (b) composites. Insets show the corresponding FESEM images.

The symbolic lines represent the experimental OA transmission data for (a) GO, (b) GQD, and (c) N-GQD (d) GO_Au, (e) GO_Ag, and (f) GO_Au_Ag, under four different input intensities of 5 (A), 12 (B), 23 (C) and 29 MW/cm2 (D). Solid/red lines show the theoretical fittings to the experimental data. (g) Variation of ln (I) with ln (1-TOA) corresponding to the OA data under 29 MW/cm2, for GO (blue), N-GQD (green) and GO_Au_Ag (black). Lines are linear fittings to the experimental data. (h) OA Z-scan data of the used solvent at various peak intensities. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Transitions related to the NLO absorptions of the composite samples. (b) The schematic representation of the atomic structure of the GO_Au_Ag.
Variation of β with input intensity (I0 ). (a) for GO, GQD, and N-GQD. (b) GO_Au, GO_Ag, and GO_Au_Ag.

The symbolic line represents the CA Z-scan transmittance for pure NLO refraction for the sample (a) GO, (b) N-GQD, and (c) GO_Au_Ag for three different input intensities (A) 5, (B) 12, and (D) 29 MW/cm2. The solid red line represents the fitting to the data with the equation (7). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optical limiting data for the samples N-GQD and GO_Au_Ag. Open symbols represent the experimental data while the solid lines are theoretical curves.
2PA absorption coefficients at different intensities under 800 nm, 150 fs pulse after solvent correction.
3PA absorption coefficients at different intensities under 800 nm, 150 fs pulse.
The coefficient of third-order NLO refraction n2 and susceptibility χ (3) under 800 nm 150 fs pulse laser excitation.
Third order NLO parameter of some previously reported nanomaterials.

row.entry: Annealed ZnO QDs in Al2O3 matrix

head.ce:title: Giant femtosecond nonlinear optical response in bi-metallic GO nanocomposites for photonic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Laboratory, Dept. of Physics, National Institute of Technology Durgapur, 713209 West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanoscience Laboratory, Dept. of Physics, National Institute of Technology Durgapur, 713209 West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Centre of Research in High Energy Materials (ACRHEM), University of Hyderabad, Hyderabad 500046, Telangana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Centre of Research in High Energy Materials (ACRHEM), University of Hyderabad

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Centre of Research in High Energy Materials (ACRHEM), University of Hyderabad, Hyderabad 500046, Telangana, India

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decades, graphene-based two-dimensional (2D) materials are emerged as bench-marked materials in photonics. The independence of the interband transitions on excitation wavelength (over a wavelength range) in graphene makes it unique and also graphene based materials are capable of exhibiting large nonlinear optical (NLO) response [1]. Owing to the intrinsic zero gap electronic band structure, electrons from the valence band (VB) in graphene can be easily lifted into the conduction band (CB) under the excitation of ultrafast pulse laser radiation. Because of this phenomenon, transiently generated carriers under intense laser irradiation may quickly occupy the energy state around the K point of the Brillouin zone. Consequently, the filling of the CB as well as the depletion of the VB may occur which may lead to the phenomenon of VB bleaching [1,2]. Now the photoinduced excited carrier in graphene relax through the processes of carrier–carrier scattering and carrier–phonon coupling (150 fs) followed by the recombination of electron-hole (2 ps). As a result, the bleaching of the VB is prolonged in case of the shorter pulse width excitation and saturable absorption (SA) takes place [3]. Particularly in GO, it has an exclusive chemical structure that contains multiple functional groups [4]. It also has a smartly designed heterogeneous electronic band-structure coming from the presence of both sp2 and sp3 C = C bonds [4]. That is why, GO delivers intriguing photo-physical properties that give us exciting prospects for tuning the NLO properties of GO. Due to the presence of those functional groups, restructuring of electronic energy bands and tuning of sp2 and sp3 domain ratio in GO can be carried out deliberately by controlling its size, doping with other elements, and formation of suiatble composites materials [5–9]. With such modifications, one can amend the relaxation process of the excited carrier that can significantly enhance the SA response. The switching of the NLO response from SA to RSA can also be achieved due to the presence of one or more than one multiphoton absorption processes [5]. The reduction of size is one of the easiest ways to tune the optical properties of GO. As the size is reduced, it suffers a strong quantum confinement effect, attributed to the occupancy of a potential region having strongly repulsive barriers by the π electron wave functions. This phenomenon can enhance the NLO response in materials like graphene quantum dot (GQD) [10,11]. Nevertheless, by incorporation of other materials in GQD, can sufficiently increase the sp3 carbon domain that can bring a more robust NLO response by stimulating two-photon absorption (2PA) phenomenon [11]. On the other hand, the composites of GO with other 2D materials are also found to exhibit improved NLO behavior. Sharma et al. has documented the significant enhancement in the 2PA coefficient in strongly coupled heterostructures of MoSe2-GO than those of its individual components, i.e., MoSe2 and GO [12]. Graphene/WS2 composites are also reported to exhibit a considerable enhancement in NLO behavior [13]. The enhancement in the NLO response is achieved in the above nanocomposites of GO with other 2D nanomaterials but the reported syntheses processes are very much tricky. This has insisted the research community to study the NLO response from the composite nanomaterials of GO with noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), which can produce immense NLO response and also syntheses processes are much easier. Liu et al. have reported eight times enhancement in NLO susceptibility in (GO)/Pt complex hybrids in compared to that of GO [14]. Also, several investigations on the NLO response of GO-Ag nanocomposite have been carried out under the excitation of different wavelengths and different pulse duration of the incident laser beam [2,15–17]. The composite of GO with Au NPs is also being investigated [18,19]. All these GO-metal nanoparticle composites demonstrate enhanced NLO response based on the modification of the relaxation process through the transfer of charge carriers between GO and metal NPs.

Noble metal NPs, specifically Au and Ag NPs are known for their interesting optical properties based on their electronic band structure. Particularly they have closely placed hybrid sp and Fermi energy levels. Thus, by integrating the band structure of both Au and Ag NPs together with the band structure of GO, modification in the band-to-band transition of electrons and their relaxation process can be achieved and this may result into development of an unique material with gigantic NLO response. Therefore, it would be exciting to see what happens to the NLO response of GO if we intuitively integrate both Au and Ag NPs with the GO nanosheet. The materials with a stronger NLO response or a giant nonlinear coefficient will require less interaction length with the incident pulse radiation and this may open up the pathway for the next level miniaturization of the photonic devices [20]. Apart from the technological applications in photonics device, high-power ultrashort pulse laser radiation with a high repetition rate is also used in the medical sector. Likely in orthopedic surgery and in treatment of malignant tissue, extreme precision is required to avoid any accidental damage. It necessitates the development of optical limiting (OL) material against such intense laser beam. Therefore, there is a constant demand for the materials having enhanced NLO responses. This can be achieved with the modified GO nanosheets, as it could be understood from the aforementioned discussions and this has inspired us for carrying out a detailed comparative study on the NLO responses of GO, GQD, N-GQD, GO_Ag, GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag, to find out a promising material with robust NLO response.

However, in this study, for the first time, we present the femtosecond NLO response of GO_Au_Ag nanocomposite, formed with GO nanosheets and Au and Ag NPs, by Z-scan technique under the excitation of 150 fs pulsed laser at 800 nm. A massive three orders of magnitude enhancement in the two-photon absorption coefficient (β) is observed when both Au and Ag NPs are incorporated intuitively in GO nanosheet in compared to those of bare GO, GQD and N-GQD samples. Here our data suggests that GO, GQD and N-GQD exhibit 2PA. However, as a consequence of increase in sp3 carbon domain, two times larger β value is observed in N-GQD. On the other hand, GO-metal nanoparticle composites exhibit coexistence of 2PA, three photon absorption (3PA), and excited state absorption (ESA). As a consequence of prolonged relaxation time in presence of both Au and Ag NPs, GO_Au_Ag sample shows enhanced SA under low-intensity excitation. Whereas, at high intensity, due to the charge transfer between GO and the metallic state through ESA, it bids more assertive RSA behavior than bare GO and its smaller counterparts i.e., GQDs and N-GQDs. The χ(3) value of GO_Au_Ag is estimated to be ∼ 10−9 esu, which is two order of magnitude larger than those of other samples. Additionally, in OL application this bi-metallic GO composite exhibits the lowest OL threshold (Fth ) of ∼ 4.6 μJ/cm2, which is almost the half of that of N-GQD sample.

In this work, initially the synthesis of GO has been carried out by the modified Hammers method [15] and then GO_Au, GO_Ag, and GO_Au_Ag nanocomposites are prepared. To prepare the GO_Au and GO_Ag composites, at first Au and Ag NPs are synthesized by reduction of the Chloroauric acid HAuCl4 (1 mM) and AgNO3 (1 mM), respectively, using sodium boro-hydrade (NaBH4) solution (1 mM) at ambient condition. After that, 300 mg of GO is added with the prepared Au and Ag NPs solutions, separately. After 30 min of vigorous stirring, the solution is put in a stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at a constant temperature of 170 °C. After 8 h, the solution is collected from the autoclave and centrifuged at 10000 rpm to extract the solid sample. After centrifugation, the remaining part of the solution is collected after washing and left to dry at ambient conditions. In the case of GO_Au_Ag composite, where both Au and Ag NPs are present with GO, at first Au and Ag NPs are synthesized as mentioned above. And then both Ag and Au NPs solutions are mixed together with 300 mg of GO by stirring and put in a stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. On the other hand, a solid-state pyrolysis method has been followed to prepare the GQD and N-GQD samples, as reported earlier by Pramanik et al., from our group [21]. All the samples are diluted further in distilled-water and used to investigate their morphologies, linear and nonlinear optical properties. The linear absorption spectra are collected in (U-3010, Hitachi) UV–Visible spectrophotometer. TEM and HRTEM images are collected using a JEOL JEM 2100F system, operated at 200 kV. However, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) data, EDS spectra, and X-ray diffraction data for GO, GO_Au, and GO_Au_Ag samples are collected in solid samples.

The successful synthesis of GO, GO_ Au, GO_Ag composite, and co-composite with both Au and Ag NPs can be confirmed from the UV–Vis. absorption spectra, presented in Fig. 1 (a i-iv). In GO its characteristic peak corresponds to the π - π * transition is appeared at 250 nm [15,19]. Whereas, for GO_Ag composite, along with the π - π * transition peak for GO, another peak centered at ∼ 420 nm (attributed to the Plasmonic absorption of Ag NPs) is present, which confirmed the presence of Ag NPs with GO. Similarly for GO_Au, the peak centered at ∼ 560 nm (attributed to the Plasmonic absorption of Au NPs) along with the π - π * transition peak for GO, confirmed the presence of Au NPs with GO. In the GO_Au_Ag composite, a broad peak is appeared in the region covering 300 nm to 600 nm due to the overlapping of the Plasmonic absorption band of Ag NPs at ∼ 420 nm and at ∼ 560 nm for Au NPs. Such broad peak and π - π * transition band of GO reflect the coexistence of GO along with the Au and Ag NPs. Furthermore, one can realize the integration of Au and Ag NPs with the GO nanosheet from the redshift of the π - π * transition band to 262 nm and 270 nm for the sample GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag, respectively [15]. In GQD, the π - π * transition peak is found to appear at 272 nm in its absorption spectrum [Fig. 1a (v)]. However, after the doping of nitrogen in GQD, it shifted to 312 nm, and its intensity is decreased [Fig. 1a (vi)]. It confirms the increment in sp3 domains in its atomic structure [21]. That has a critical role in the NLO response of graphene-based nanomaterials.

To collect more evidence about the successful formation of composite materials XRD measurement has been carried and the results are presented in Fig. 1 (b). In GO, the XRD spectra showed the presence of only peak at ∼ 13°, attributed to the reflection coming from the (001) plane [15] and the corresponding interplanar spacing is 0.680 nm. However, this peak is shifted to the lower 2θ value (∼10°) and the interplanar spacing is increased to 0.884 nm in both GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag composite samples. This indicates the fact that the Au and Ag NPs do not just exist in the solution with GO nanosheets but the composite has been formed successfully. Both the GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag samples showed the presence of four more peaks in their XRD data, at 38.6°, 44.8°, 64.8° and 75.5°, attributed to the diffraction from (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of the face-centered cubic structured Au and Ag NPs [15]. However, these peaks are more intense in GO_Au_Ag than GO_Au because of the presence of both the Au and Ag NPs with GO.

The TEM characterizations have also been done for further information on morphology of the samples. The TEM image of GO has been depicted in Fig. 2 (a), which shows the successful synthesis of GO nanosheet. Fig. 2 (b) shows the TEM image of the GO_Au composite, in which the integration of Au NPs with the GO nanosheet can be easily observed. The average particle size is estimated to be around 60 nm, calculated using different images taken from the different parts of the sample grid. Ideally the AuNPs with smaller size (5 ∼ 10 nm) show the Plasmonic absorption band in its UV–vis. absorption spectrum at around 500 nm. But in the present case, because of the large particle size, the Plasmonic band is red-shifted to around 560 nm (Fig. 1 a-iii). The TEM image of the GO_Ag and GO_Au_Ag composite is being shown in Fig. 2 (c) and (d), respectively.

In Fig. 2(d), the presence of both the smaller and bigger size metal NPs can be observed. With the analogy to the UV–vis. absorption spectra of the sample of GO_Au_Ag composite, the smaller particles are Ag NPs, and the bigger particles are Au NPs. Furthermore, the EDS spectra of GO_Au is shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the same for GO_Au_Ag is presented in Fig. 3 (b). These EDS spectra showed enough evidence about the presence of only Au NPs and both Au and Ag NPs in the synthesized GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag composites, respectively. The insets of Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show the corresponding FESEM images of GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag composites, respectively.

The TEM image of the synthesized GQD is presented in Fig. 2 (e). The average size of the synthesized GQDs is found to be around 10 nm. From the HRTEM image of GQD [Fig. 2 (f)], the inter planner distance is found to be ∼ 0.31 nm which matches with the (100) plane of graphene [21]. The TEM image of N-GQD is shown in Fig. 2(g), where the average particle size is reduced to around 5 nm. Furthermore, from the enlarged view of a single N-GQD [Fig. 2 (h)], the distortion in the lattice plane can be observed, which indicates the introduction of lattice defects due to N doping [21].

The NLO properties of all samples are investigated by using the well-known Z-scan technique under both OA and CA conditions. Here the samples are excited with the femtosecond (800 nm, 150 fs) laser pulse with different intensities, at a repetition rate of 80 MHz. The OA transmission data for all samples are presented in Fig. 4 (a) GO, (b) GQD, (c) N-GQD, (d) GO_Au, (e) GO_Ag, and (f) GO_Au_Ag.
(1)
dI ( r , z , t )
d
z
'
= -
[
α 0
I
r , z , t
+ β
I
2
r , z , t
+ γ
I
3
r , z , t
+ ⋯ ]

The nonlinear interaction of the incident light beam with the optically thin medium can be followed with the Eqn. (1) [22]. Here, α0 is the linear absorption coefficient. In Eqn. (1), β and γ represent the 2PA and 3PA coefficients, respectively. In case only 2PA is present in the medium the value of β can be evaluated by fitting the OA Z-scan data with the solution of the above Eqn. (1) as follows [22],
(2)
T
Z
=
∑
n = 0
∞
-
L
eff
( n + 1 )
3 / 2
n
β I
( 1 +
Z
2
/
Z
0
2
)
n

Where,
L
eff
n
=
1 - e x p
1 - n
α 0
L
/
( n - 1 )
α 0
, the effective path length of the medium travelled by the interacting light pulse and L is the length of the medium.

Fig. 4 clearly shows that in all samples, at lower input intensity, the output transmittance is increased as it travels towards the focus, which is the typical negative (-ve) nonlinear absorption or SA behaviour. But as the intensity is increased, such negative NLO response is diminished, and at higher input intensity positive NLO response or RSA behaviour is observed. The OA Z-scan transmittance data of GO, GQD and N-GQD are fitted very well to Eqn. (2), which revealed that such NLO response is attributed to the 2PA phenomenon in the materials.

However, for both the composite materials of GO_Au and GO_Au_Ag, the shape of the OA Z-scan transmittance data under different intensities indicates the presence of more than one nonlinear absorption phenomena. In such a situation, Eqn. (1) can be written as [23],
(3)
dI
dz
= -
α 0
I
1 + I /
I S
-
β I
2
1 +
I
2
I
S 2 P A
2
- γ
I
3

Where, IS and IS2PA is the saturation intensity for the 1PA and 2PA, respectively and here the simultaneous presences of linear or one photon absorption (1PA), 2PA and 3PA have been considered. Thus the total absorption coefficient α(I) of the sample can be modeled as [23],
(4)
α
I
=
α 0
1 + I /
I s
+
β I
1 +
I
2
I
S 2 P A
2
+ γ
I
2

It is challenging to solve Eqn. (4) to get the absorption coefficient value. However, as reported earlier, one can evaluate the value of the β and γ by fitting the OA Z-scan transmittance data with Eqn. (2) by replacing the βI/(1 + Z2/Z0 2) with the Eqn. (4) [24,25].

Interestingly, the OA Z-scan data for GO_Au, GO_Ag, and GO_Au_Ag composite samples are fitted successfully using the method as mentioned above, indicating the presence of 1PA, 2PA, and 3PA in their NLO absorption. The NLO absorption coefficients are extracted from the fitting parameters. The presence of 2PA and 3PA processes can also be confirmed by plotting ln(1 − TOA) against ln(I), where I = I0 /(1 + Z2/Z0 2 ). It is well established fact that the slope (m) of the linear plot of ln(1 − TOA) vs. ln(I) confirms the presence of (m + 1) photon absorption process in the OA Z-scan experiment [15,26]. In present investigation we have plotted ln(1 − TOA) vs. ln(I) for three samples, namely GO, N-GQD and GO_Au_Ag, as shown in Fig. 4 (g). The value of m is found out to be 1.1 and 1.0 in case of GO and N-GQD, respectively, indicating that 2PA process is the primary reason for the observation of NLO responses in GO and N-GQD. However, for GO_Au_Ag the value of m is found out to be 1.8, which confirmed the presence of 3PA in its NLO absorption behavior.

In this investigation, 150 fs laser pulse with a high repetition rate of 80 MHz is used. So it is initially suspected that thermal effect might dominate in the observed NLO responses. However, recently, Dey et al. have reported that a laser pulse of 82 MHz repetition rate with fs pulse duration has a minimal thermal effect [27]. Furthermore, to minimize the thermal impact on the NLO response of the synthesized samples, the NLO response of the solvent used to dissolve the samples is also being investigated (presented in Fig. 4h) and it is subtracted from the (solvent + sample) data. And all the values of the NLO absorption coefficients after solvent correction are tabulated in Tables 1 and 2
. This table indicates that depending on the β value; all the synthesized samples can be arranged as GO < GQD < N-GQD < GO_Au < GO_Ag < GO_Au_Ag in the SA region.

By reducing the size of GO (i e., GQD), enhancement in NLO absorption has been achieved, which is consistent with the previous report [11]. Furthermore, enhancement in the β value is being observed with the nitrogen doping to the GQD. And interestingly, by making a composite of GO with noble metal nanoparticles, a significant enhancement in the β value is achieved. Three orders of magnitude larger β value in compared to that of GO has been observed in GO_Au_Ag sample. Furthermore, the presence of 3PA with 2PA is also being observed in the GO composites with the metal nanoparticles. Such NLO absorption response can be realized by the electronic transitions involved [illustrated in Fig. 5 (a i-iii)]. GO absorbs two photons simultaneously from the incident laser beam of 800 nm (1.55 eV) through the n → π * transition band located at around 400 nm (3.1 eV), as it can be observed as a shoulder like peak in its UV–Vis. absorption spectrum [Fig. 1 (a i)]. In GQD and N-GQD they absorb it through their active band present at ∼ 400 nm (3.1 eV). And because of the enhancement in the sp3 carbon domain, they show higher NLO absorption than GO. As the Au NPs are incorporated with the GO nanosheet along with the 2PA, 3PA also takes place through the interband transition 5d → 6sp in Au NPs, as shown in Fig. 5 (a-ii). For GO composite with the Au and Ag NPs, the energy bands of the Au and Ag NPs get extended into the band structure of GO. And consequently, the relaxation process of the excited state carriers gets modified. To explain the NLO responses from the GO-metal nanoparticles composites we have followed an energy band model, reported earlier for Au and Ag NPs [28–30].

As shown in Fig. 5a(i) that in bare GO, 2PA excited carriers come down to the ground state directly. But in case of the composite samples, 2PA excited carriers at the π * state of GO follows the path (B-C), as mentioned in Fig. 5a(ii-iii), to reach the lower-level of GO. Consequently, excited carriers suffer more delay in the composite samples in compared to that in pure GO. Therefore, SA behavior is exhibited by the samples because of the large lifetime of the carriers in the excited state. And also because of the prolonged relaxation time the 2PA saturation behaviour in GO_Au, GO_Ag, and GO_Au_Ag samples gets enhanced. Furthermore, in GO_Ag sample an active surface Plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption band is present at ∼ 400 nm [Fig. 1 (a ii)], which also contribute in 2PA process. But the SPR band is placed almost at the same energy to that of the n → π * transition band of GO. And the 2PA excited carriers in Ag NPs can easily move towards the GO. As a consequence strong Plasmon bleaching may have taken places, leading to the larger β value in GO_Ag than that of GO_Au sample.

In the GO_Au composite, the 3PA excited carriers in 6sp state follow the path (A-B-C-D) as indicated in Fig. 5a(ii) to transit down to 5d state. In this process the 3PA excited carrier in Au first comes to the π * state of GO (via path A). From this π * state it jumps to the energy level of Au around its Fermi level (via path B) and then it comes to the lower level of GO (via path C). From here, it finally moves down to the 5d level of Au (via path D) [31]. Furthermore, the relaxation of the 3PA excited carrier of Au also gets prolonged because of the charge transfer between Au to GO as mentioned above. This enhanced the ground state's bleaching that is reflected in the improved SA behavior of GO_Au sample.

When Ag NPs are also integrated along with Au NPs with the GO sheet, 3PA excited carriers suffer longer delays, as 6sp band of Ag NPs is present just under the 6sp band of Au NPs. In this scenario, the 3PA excited carriers at the 6sp state follow the path (A’-A’’-B-C-D) and comes down to the 5d state of Au as shown in Fig. 5a (iii). Consequently, significant enhancement in the SA behavior is observed in GO_Au_Ag. At higher input intensity a large number of 2PA induced free carriers in the conduction band must have suffered excited state absorption (ESA). Because of the dominance of ESA by the free carriers, the NLO absorption response may switch to RSA from SA [1,32]. However, the dependences of β on input intensity I0 for all the samples are shown in Fig. 6 .

It can be observed that for the samples of GO, GQD and N-GQD at higher intensity the value of β remains almost constant, indicating the presence of pure 2PA in NLO absorption in these three samples. However, for GO_Au_Ag sample, the linear dependency of β on the input intensity indicates the presence of ESA in its NLO absorption behavior [1,33]. Such enhanced SA response at lower intensities can be utilized in the Q-switching and mode-locking of the laser beam. At the same time, improved RSA behavior at higher intensities can be utilized in optical switching and optical limiting applications.

Moreover, it is evident that N-GQD shows better NLO absorption response among the quantum dot samples. And GO_Au_Ag shows a much stronger NLO absorption response among all samples. That is why the CA Z-scan measurements are being performed with the GO, N-GQD, and GO_Au_Ag samples to study their NLO refraction responses [shown in Fig. 7 (a-c)].

In the case of CA Z-scan transmission data, it consists of both the nonlinear absorption and refraction. However, we can get the pure NLO refraction data by using the following mathematical approaches [34], in which we can write,
(5)
T
refraction
Z
=
T
CA
( Z )
T
OA
( Z )
p
.

Here, p is a constant which depends on the linear transmittance of the aperture used in the CA Z-scan measurement as
p = S + 0.63
( 1 - S )
2
.
The CA Z-scan transmittance data of those three samples related to the pure NLO refraction are presented in Fig. 7.

After isolating the NLO refraction data, the corresponding value of the coefficient of NLO refraction n2 is calculated by fitting the data with the Eqn. (6), which is given as [34],
(6)
T
Refraction
Z
= 1 +
4
Z /
Z 0

Z 0
2
+ 9

2
+ 1
Δ

0
( 3 )

Where,
Δ

0
( 3 )
= 2 π
n 2
I 0
L
eff
/ λ
, is the third-order nonlinear phase change in the material while interacting with the intense pulsed laser beam. The obtained value of the n2 from the fitting parameter is being presented in Table 3 after solvent correction.

Fig. 7 (a-c) indicate that at the lower intensity, the change in the third-order NLO phase is -ve, but as intensity is increased it is switched to + ve in all three samples. However, from Table 3, one can observe that in both N-GQD and GO_Au_Ag samples, we have achieved one order higher value of n2 than that of only GO. Nevertheless, a two times larger value of n2 for the GO_Au_Ag composite sample than that of N-GQD proves the dominance of the composite nanomaterial of GO with both Au and Ag NPs over the modified quantum dot materials. Such enhancement in the NLO refraction may have occurred due to the fact that the NLO refraction coefficient is a function of the free carrier density of the medium [35]. And in case of GO_Au_Ag, as discussed earlier, the free carrier generated through the multi-photon absorption process suffers more delay in the relaxation process in the presence of Au and Ag NPs. This increases the free carrier density in the material, and as a consequence NLO refraction coefficient is increased. More bound electrons in the sp3 domain are uplifted from VB to the CB at higher input intensity. Such redistribution of the carrier population gives rise to the excited state carrier population leading to the enhancement in the excited states refraction. Such excited state refraction and contribution from bound electrons or π electrons of the VB in the NLO refraction response result in the switching of NLO refraction from self-defocusing to self-focusing [36]. Also Ag and Au NPs are known for their enhanced local field effect. Such local field effect may have contributed in the NLO response of the GO-metal NPs composite samples but it is mainly attributed to the modification in the relaxation process of the excited carrier in presence of Au and Ag NPs.

Moreover, the NLO refraction originated from the population redistribution and deflections of the bound charge cloud i.e., electronic Kerr effect, are very fast processes with a few tens/hundreds of fs response time. On the contrary, thermal lensing arising from the thermo-optic effect is much slower process with response time in ns or even in longer time scale regime. Consequently, in our study under fs pulse excitation thermal lensing can be ignored. Moreover, the thermally generated NLO refraction arises due to two main causes, (i) firstly due to generation of acoustic wave and (ii) the secondly due to the accumulation of heat energy followed by diffusion [37]. In case of longer pulse excitation, a part of the incident high intensity beam is absorbed by the material and gives rise to the temperature gradient within the material. This phenomenon eventually produced the acoustic wave which may contribute to the NLO refraction, if the transit time of that acoustic wave (tacoustic = ω 0/vs , here, vs is the speed at which the sound wave propagates inside the material) is larger than the pulse width of the exciting laser beam. In case of fs pulse laser excitation, this thermal lensing effect may contribute to the NLO refraction, if the repetation rate of the exciting pulse is high [37]. To avoid such thermal lensing or cumulative thermal diffusion contribution one has to take care about the characteristic time or thermal relaxation time (tc)
[ t c =
ω 0 2
ρ
C p
/ 4 κ
, where ρ, Cp , and K are the density, specific heat, and thermal conductivity of the material, respectively] [38,39]. In the used experimental condition, the calculated value of tc is found to be 8 ns which is just below the spatial interval of 12 ns of the excitation laser pulses, indicating a minimal contribution to NLO refraction due to thermal lensing.

Finally, the value of χ(3) is being calculated by using the β and n2 values for the samples of GO, N-GQD and GO_Au_Ag, by using the following relations [40],
(7)
χ
3
( S I )
=
(
2
ε 0
n
0
2
c
3
)
( 2
n 2
+ i β λ / 2 π )

The obtained value of | χ (3)| (esu) [= | χ (3) | (SI) × 108 /1.4] is presented in Table 3. In the case of both, GO and N-GQD, χ (3) value of the order of 10−11 (esu) is obtained. Here it is important to note that an enhancement by a factor of 2 in χ (3) (esu) value is obtained in N-GQDs in compared to that of pure GO. But, for the sample of GO_Au_Ag, a massive two orders of magnitude enhancement in χ (3) (∼10−9 esu) is reported.

In the Table 4 , we have summarized the NLO results of some recently reported nanomaterials having good NLO response along with those obtained in this work in GO_Ag_Au sample. This Table 4 also clearly shows that GO_Au_Ag is showing the best performance among all the materials. So, it is evident that to get the most robust NLO response, integration or loading of Au NPs and Ag NPs together in GO nanosheet is much more favorable than its size reduction.

Presently, laser pulses with high repetition rates are used in the medical sector and it has become necessary to develop OL materials to avoid any accidental damage to human and optical components on exposure to highly intense laser beams. Here, we have studied the OL response for the sample of N-GQD and GO_Au_Ag; those are observed to have higher NLO responses. In this investigation, the input fluence (Fin ) is calculated corresponding to the positions of the sample in the OA Z-scan measurement by using the relation below [34].
(8)
F
in
= 4
( l n 2 )
1 / 2
E
in
/
π
3 / 2
ω
0
2
( 1 +
Z
2
/
Z
0
2
)
.

Then the transmittance at each point is being measured for different Fin , the results are presented in Fig. 8 . The Fig. 8 indicates that the OL for the N-GQD sample begins (onset point) at the input fluence of 2.3 μJ/cm2.

And the extended graph shows that it has an OL threshold (Fth ) of 8.2 μJ/cm2, where the output fluence dropped by 50 % of the input fluence. On the otherhand, in GO_Au_Ag composite the onset and threshold of OL are reduced to just 1.7 μJ/cm2 and 4.6 μJ/cm2, respectively which is almost half to that of N-GQD sample. Previously, ZnFe2O4–rGO and Ag-Fe2O3–rGO nanocomposite is reported to have larger values of OL onset of 14.7 and 29.44 μJ/cm2, respectively [5,49]. Ag nanowire is reported to have a large Fth value of 6.2 μJ/cm2 [48]. Ethylenediamminium Picrate crystal has also been reported to have a large value of OL onset [54]. Therefore, it is evident that the loading of Au NPs and Ag NPs together in GO gives a much better response in limiting the incoming intense laser beam. And the OL performance of GO_Au_Ag sample is superior to those of earlier reports [5,49,54] on Ag nanowire, ZnFe2O4–rGO nanocomposite and Ethylenediamminium Picrate crystal.

The observation of the good OL response of GO_Au_Ag sample coupled with the observations of higher-order optical nonlinearity associated with the three or more photon absorptions may bring many more advantages [55]. The dependency of the output signal on the higher order of the input intensity provides higher penetration depth to improve the transparency of living cells and enhances the image resolution when used in multi-photon absorption-based bio-medical imaging [55–57]. Here we have observed that the synthesized sample of GO_Au_Ag shows a strong 3PA with γ value of the order of 10−12 (cm3/W2) thus it may be very useful in multiphoton based imaging applications in future. Therefore, the prepared nanocomposite of GO with both Au NPs and Ag NPs (i.e., GO_Au_Ag) may provide a weighty contribution to achieving significant advancement in the field of applications of NLO materials in photonic devices.

For the first time, herein we have reported the investigations on NLO responses of some nanocomposite materials of GO nanosheet with noble bi-metals by fs OA and CA Z-scan techniques and observed the coexistence of 2PA and 3PA, originated from the n- π * transition in GO and 5d-6sp transition in Au. In this comparative study, NLO responses of five different graphene-based nanomaterials like GO, its smaller counterpart GQD and N-doped GQD, composite of GO with Au NPs, as well as composite of GO with both Au and Ag NPs are investigated. All the samples showed intensity dependent switching of NLO absorption from SA to RSA, and switching of photoinduced third-order NLO phase from self-defocusing to self-focusing. However, two orders of magnitude larger β value and one order of magnitude larger n2 value are obtained in GO_Au_Ag nanocomposite in comparison to those of others. It is found that making a composite of GO with two noble metal nanoparticles (namely Au and Ag NPs) is much more favorable to get a robust NLO response than reducing its size i.e., GQD formation as well as doping in GQD. Moreover, the extraordinary value of χ (3) of the order of 10−9 (esu) and OL threshold of just 4.6 μJ/cm2 proves the applicability and superiority of the synthesized GO_Au_Ag nanocomposite for its future use in the vast field of photonic applications of NLO materials.

Koushik Mondal: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Subrata Biswas: Data curation, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Ashim Pramanik: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Dipanjan Banerjee: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Venugopal Rao Soma: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Anil Kumar Chaudhary: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Pathik Kumbhakar: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zou, C. Hu, H. Zhou, S. Li, X. Luo, J. He, C. Yuan, Structure modulation in confined nanoparticles: the role of the strain gradient. J. Phys. Chem. C 124 (2020) 21810−21817.
